title: Lesson 2 - Designing a Skill Artifact
description: Translate your charter into a concrete folder structure, master SKILL.md frontmatter, and understand how progressive disclosure keeps skills fast and maintainable.
learningObjectives: Create a correctly named skill directory with a valid SKILL.md file.
Author compliant YAML frontmatter with name and description (required) plus optional governance fields.
Structure the instruction body with scannable sections that map to the four-part hierarchy.
Decide when to add scripts/, references/, and assets/ without bloating SKILL.md.

videos.title: From Charter to Folder
videos.description: Live-build the minimum viable skill folder, validate naming conventions, and watch the AI tool recognize it instantly.
scriptOutline: Recap the charter from Lesson 1 and derive the folder name (kebab-case, <=64 chars).
Create the folder + empty SKILL.md. Show the directory where it will live.
Explain naming pitfalls: spaces, uppercase, underscores, version suffixes, consecutive hyphens.
Live demo: open Claude Code (or Copilot) and watch it recognize the new skill instantly.
Running example: create the deploy-checklist/ folder and commit the skeleton to git.
talkingPoints: A skill name should read like a clear command: 'deploy-checklist', 'meeting-notes', 'code-review'.
The folder name IS the skill name — they must match exactly.
Version control from day one: commit the skeleton so changes are tracked.

videos.title: SKILL.md Frontmatter Deep Dive
videos.description: Walk through every field, explain why descriptions must be scenario-specific, and show what makes the AI activate your skill.
scriptOutline: The two parts of SKILL.md: frontmatter (metadata between --- markers) and body (instructions).
Required fields: name (must match folder name) and description (the AI reads this to decide when to activate).
Optional standard fields: license, compatibility, metadata.
Writing great descriptions: answer 'When should the AI use this?' not 'What does this skill do?'.
Live demo: weak description vs. strong description — show how activation accuracy changes.
Running example: write the deploy-checklist frontmatter with a scenario-specific description.
talkingPoints: Think of frontmatter as the label on a jar — the AI reads it to decide whether to open the jar.
A vague description like 'helps with deployments' is useless. 'Run pre-deploy validations before shipping backend services' is actionable.
The description is the most important line in your entire skill. Spend real time on it.

videos.title: Instruction Body and Supporting Directories
videos.description: Structure the markdown body with placeholder sections and learn when to offload content into supporting directories.
scriptOutline: Sketch the four default sections (Trigger, Workflow, Constraints, Output) as placeholders — we'll populate them in Lesson 3.
Show how markdown headings and numbered lists make instructions parsable for both AI and humans.
Introduce optional directories: scripts/ (automation), references/ (templates/examples), assets/ (data/configs).
Progressive disclosure: AI reads metadata first, loads instructions when activated, reads files on demand.
Decision thresholds: >500 lines in SKILL.md? Move templates to references/. Repeating shell tasks? Add scripts/.
Running example: add the four placeholder sections to deploy-checklist/SKILL.md.
talkingPoints: Structure before content: even a placeholder skeleton prevents rambling instructions later.
Supporting directories are appendices — reference them explicitly from SKILL.md.
Progressive disclosure keeps the AI fast: it only loads what it needs, when it needs it.
Most skills start with just SKILL.md. Don't add directories until you actually need them.

textSections.title: Canonical Skill Layout
textSections.content:
Here's the complete anatomy of a skill:

```
deploy-checklist/              # Folder name = skill name (kebab-case)
├── SKILL.md                   # Required — frontmatter + instructions
├── references/                # Optional — templates, examples, style guides
│   ├── template.md
│   └── example-good.md
├── scripts/                   # Optional — automation scripts
│   └── run-sanity-check.sh
└── assets/                    # Optional — data files, configs, images
    └── release-matrix.json
```

**Only SKILL.md is required.** Everything else is optional. Start minimal, add directories when you need them.

textSections.title: Complete SKILL.md Example: deploy-checklist
textSections.content:
Here is the running example skill we'll build across this course:

```markdown
---
name: deploy-checklist
description: Run pre-deploy validations before shipping backend services to production. Checks migrations, tests, environment variables, and team notifications.
---

# Deploy Checklist

## Trigger
Activate when the user is about to deploy backend services to production or staging.
Do NOT activate for frontend-only changes or documentation updates.

## Workflow
1. Check for pending database migrations — list any unapplied migrations.
2. Run the test suite — report pass/fail with summary.
3. Verify environment variables — confirm all required vars are set for the target environment.
4. Check for uncommitted changes — warn if the working directory is dirty.
5. Generate a deployment summary for the team notification.

## Constraints
- Never run destructive commands (DROP, DELETE, rm -rf) during validation.
- Do not proceed if tests fail — stop and report the failures.
- Do not modify any source files — this skill only validates and reports.

## Output
Return a markdown checklist:

### Deploy Readiness Report
- [ ] Migrations: [status]
- [ ] Tests: [pass/fail count]
- [ ] Env vars: [status]
- [ ] Clean working directory: [yes/no]
- [ ] Summary: [one-line description of what's being deployed]
```

This is a complete, working skill. We'll refine it over the next five lessons.

textSections.title: Frontmatter Field Reference
textSections.content:
**Required fields (open standard):**

| Field | Rules | Example |
|-------|-------|---------|
| `name` | Lowercase + hyphens, max 64 chars, must match folder name | `deploy-checklist` |
| `description` | Max 1024 chars, explains WHEN to use the skill | `Run pre-deploy validations before shipping backend services` |

**Optional fields (open standard):**

| Field | Purpose | Example |
|-------|---------|--------|
| `license` | License for sharing | `MIT` |
| `compatibility` | Platforms tested + requirements | `Tested on Claude Code, Copilot` |
| `metadata` | Arbitrary key-value pairs | `author: Jane Doe` |

**Note:** Some platforms add extra fields (Lesson 6 covers these). Always use the standard fields first.

textSections.title: Progressive Disclosure: How AI Loads Skills
textSections.content:
Your AI doesn't read every skill fully at startup — that would be slow. Instead, it uses **three-stage loading**:

**Stage 1 — Metadata (~100 tokens):** At startup, the AI reads only `name` and `description` from frontmatter. This is how it knows what skills exist.

**Stage 2 — Instructions (<5,000 tokens):** When a task matches a skill's description, the AI loads the full SKILL.md body. Now it has detailed instructions.

**Stage 3 — Resources (on demand):** Only when referenced by the instructions, the AI reads files from `scripts/`, `references/`, or `assets/`.

This is why a great `description` matters so much — it's the gateway to everything else.

textSections.title: Supporting Directory Decision Tree
textSections.content:
Ask these questions before adding a directory:

| Question | If Yes → | Example |
|----------|---------|--------|
| Am I pasting the same template into SKILL.md? | Move it to `references/` | Output template, style guide |
| Do I need repeatable automation? | Create a `scripts/` file | Lint checker, test runner |
| Does the AI need static data to do its job? | Store it in `assets/` | JSON config, CSV data |
| Is SKILL.md over 400 lines? | Split content into supporting files | Long examples → references/ |

**Default answer: not yet.** Start with just SKILL.md. Add directories when you feel the pain.

commonMistakes.mistake: Folder name doesn't match the name field
commonMistakes.explanation: If your folder is 'code-review/', the name field MUST be 'code-review'. Any mismatch makes the skill invisible to your AI tool.
'A helpful deployment skill' tells the AI nothing. Write: 'Run pre-deploy validations before shipping backend services to production.' The AI uses the description to decide WHEN to activate.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding directories before you need them
commonMistakes.explanation: An empty scripts/ folder adds complexity without value. Start with just SKILL.md. Add scripts/ when you actually have a script to put there.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using uppercase, spaces, or underscores in the skill name
commonMistakes.explanation: Skill names must be lowercase with hyphens: 'deploy-checklist' not 'Deploy_Checklist' or 'deploy checklist'.

commonMistakes.mistake: Putting everything in one massive SKILL.md
commonMistakes.explanation: If SKILL.md exceeds 500 lines, it becomes hard to scan and maintain. Move templates to references/, data to assets/, and automation to scripts/.

reflectionQuestions: Look at the complete deploy-checklist example. Which section is clearest? Which would you write differently?
For your chartered workflow, is SKILL.md alone enough, or do you already know you'll need supporting files?
How would you describe your skill in one sentence so the AI knows EXACTLY when to activate it?
What's the difference between a description that says what the skill does vs. when to use it?

exercises.title: Build Your Skill Skeleton
exercises.description: Create the folder for your chartered workflow. Add SKILL.md with valid frontmatter and the four placeholder sections (Trigger, Workflow, Constraints, Output).
exercises.output: A folder with SKILL.md containing frontmatter + placeholder section headings.
successCriteria: Folder name is lowercase kebab-case, under 64 characters.
SKILL.md frontmatter has name (matching folder) and a scenario-specific description.
Four placeholder sections (Trigger, Workflow, Constraints, Output) are present.
Committed to git with a descriptive commit message.

exercises.title: Description Stress Test
exercises.description: Write three different descriptions for your skill. Test each by asking: 'Would the AI activate this skill for the right task and NOT activate it for the wrong task?'
exercises.output: Three candidate descriptions with notes on which is most precise.
successCriteria: Three descriptions that differ meaningfully, not just in wording.
Evaluated each against a 'should activate' scenario and a 'should NOT activate' scenario.
Selected the strongest description and updated SKILL.md.

exercises.title: Reverse-Engineer an Existing Skill
exercises.description: Find a community skill (github.com/anthropics/skills or similar). Map each file to the canonical layout diagram. Note how they use frontmatter and supporting files.
exercises.output: Annotated breakdown of another team's skill structure.
successCriteria: Identified every frontmatter field in the reference skill.
Documented how the instruction body links to supporting files.
Listed one idea to adopt and one thing you'd do differently.

instructorNotes: This lesson introduces the most 'technical' concepts (file structure, YAML). Reassure non-tech learners: creating a folder and a text file is something everyone can do.
If learners find YAML intimidating, compare it to a simple form: 'name: value' — that's all frontmatter is.
Live demo tip: build the deploy-checklist skeleton in real time, showing each keystroke. Then open your AI tool and show it recognizing the skill immediately.
The complete deploy-checklist example is the anchor for this lesson. Walk through every line and explain the reasoning.
Progressive disclosure is abstract — use the library analogy: you read the book title first, then the back cover, then open the book only if it's relevant.
For Exercise 2 (description stress test): this is where learners discover that writing is harder than it looks. Celebrate the struggle — it's the most valuable exercise.
